title: Google TPU 8t and 8i is here..
summary: Google released TPU 8t for training and TPU 8i for inference, served via Virgo Network for high throughput. The video compares these ASICs with general-purpose GPUs and discusses how scaling strategies from major vendors affect token economics and LLM serving costs.
writeup: Google's TPU lags Nvidia on raw per-chip compute, but this analysis reframes the comparison at infrastructure scale, where Google's 4x4x4 cube topology and ICI interconnects let 9,600 AT chips form a superpod delivering 121 exaflops in FP4, outpacing Nvidia's Vera Rubin pod at 60 exaflops. The through-line is that training and inference have opposite demand profiles (network-bandwidth-bound vs. compute-bound vs. memory-latency-bound), which explains both why Google bifurcated the TPU into AT and 8i chips and why Nvidia's more balanced pod architecture targets a broader customer base rather than maximizing peak scale.
key_points: Google's 9,600-chip AT superpod delivers 121 exaflops in FP4, more than double Nvidia's Vera Rubin pod at 60 exaflops in FP4, despite the AT chip being slower than Nvidia silicon on a per-chip basis; the gap closes because Google's ICI optical interconnect and 4x4x4 cube topology reduce scaling overhead.

A SemiAnalysis demand-profile analysis shows LLM training is primarily network-bandwidth-bound (not compute or memory-bound), while inference splits into a compute-bound prefill stage and a memory-bandwidth-plus-network-latency-bound decode stage, driving Google to ship two separate chips rather than one general-purpose design.

Google's Virgo network extends scale-out to 134,000 chips today with a stated path to 1 million chips in a single fabric, directly targeting the network-bandwidth bottleneck that Jensen Huang has positioned Nvidia to own in the inference era.

Nvidia's pod architecture reflects a deliberately balanced profile because its customer base ranges from neo-clouds like CoreWeave to frontier labs like OpenAI and xAI to hyperscalers, each with different demand profiles; Google can optimize more aggressively because TPU demand has historically been internal and predictable.

Anthropic signed a $1 billion TPU deal with Google, allocating 40% to Anthropic's own datacenter and renting the remaining 60% from Google, reflecting how frontier labs are betting on TPU infrastructure as a serious alternative to GPU clusters.

Epoch AI projects more than 450 new pretrained models by 2030, including over 100 models at or above GPT-5 scale (estimated at 66 septillion floating-point operations); at 121 exaflops in FP4, Google's superpod could theoretically complete a GPT-5-scale pretraining run in under 7 days, though practical runs accounting for efficiency losses land closer to 3 to 4 weeks.
